hero.tagline: Standard $5,000 cap with informal proceedings and no counterclaims allowed.

at_a_glance.label: Most you can sue for
at_a_glance.detail: Same cap for individuals and businesses

at_a_glance.label: Court
at_a_glance.value: Small Claims Department of the Magistrate's Division

at_a_glance.label: Appeal window
at_a_glance.detail: Trial de novo in district court

at_a_glance.label: Recent change
at_a_glance.detail: No major changes in the last 3 years

limits.split_claims_explanation: If a claim exceeds the $5,000 cap, you cannot split it into multiple claims to fit within the limit. Instead, you must file in a higher court.

claim_categories.title: Contracts and money owed
claim_categories.blurb: Idaho allows you to file claims for breaches of contract and money owed up to $5,000. The statute of limitations varies depending on the type of contract.

claims.name: Breach of a written contract
claims.example: A contractor signed a $4,000 contract to remodel your kitchen, took the deposit, and never finished.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the signed contract, payment proof, and any correspondence showing the breach.

claims.name: Breach of an oral contract
claims.example: You agreed verbally to lend someone $1,000, and they failed to repay it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from breach. Provide evidence of the agreement and any partial payments.

claims.name: Unpaid personal loan
claims.example: You loaned a friend $2,500 with the understanding they'd repay in six months, but they haven't.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Document the loan agreement and any repayment attempts.

claims.name: Default on a promissory note
claims.example: Someone signed a promissory note to repay $3,000 but missed payments.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the original promissory note and payment records.

claims.name: Unpaid invoice for services rendered
claims.example: You provided $1,200 worth of consulting services, but the client hasn't paid the invoice.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Include the invoice, service agreement, and communication records.

claims.name: Unpaid balance on an open account
claims.example: A customer has an outstanding balance of $4,500 on their account with your business.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide account statements and records of transactions.

claims.name: Unpaid rent by tenant
claims.example: Your tenant owes $3,000 in back rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Payment with a bad check
claims.example: A customer paid with a $500 check that bounced due to insufficient funds.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of check. Provide the bounced check and any bank notices.
claims.damage_boost: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

claims.name: Co-signer seeking repayment
claims.example: You co-signed a $4,000 loan, and the primary borrower defaulted, leaving you responsible.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Present the loan agreement and proof of your payments.

claim_categories.title: Consumer disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Idaho allows consumers to file claims up to $5,000 for issues like defective products or poor services. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective product
claims.example: You bought a $1,200 appliance that stopped working within a month, and the seller refuses to repair or replace it.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from date of purchase. Bring the purchase receipt and any warranty information.

claims.name: Substandard service provided
claims.example: You paid $800 for a home cleaning service that did not perform as agreed.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from service date. Provide the service agreement and evidence of poor service.

claims.name: Faulty auto repair work
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,500 for repairs that didn't fix the issue.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Bring repair invoices and any expert evaluations.

claims.name: Contractor failed to complete work
claims.example: You paid a contractor $4,000 to remodel your bathroom, but they abandoned the project halfway.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide the contract, payment records, and photos of incomplete work.

claims.name: Vendor failed to deliver goods
claims.example: You paid $2,000 for furniture that was never delivered.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Bring the purchase agreement and payment proof.

claims.name: Unauthorized subscription charges
claims.example: A company continued to charge your credit card $50 monthly after you canceled the subscription.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from unauthorized charge. Provide cancellation confirmation and billing statements.

claims.name: Purchase of a defective used car
claims.example: You bought a used car for $4,500 that broke down within a week, and the seller refuses to refund.
claims.notes: 4-year deadline from purchase. Bring the bill of sale and any repair records.

claims.name: False advertising leading to financial loss
claims.example: A company advertised a product for $300 with features it didn't have, leading you to purchase it under false pretenses.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from discovery. Provide the advertisement and purchase records.

claim_categories.title: Property damage
claim_categories.blurb: Claims for property damage up to $5,000 can be filed in Idaho's small claims court. The statute of limitations is generally 3 years.

claims.name: Damage from a vehicle collision
claims.example: Another driver hit your parked car, causing $2,500 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of accident. Bring the police report and repair estimates.

claims.name: Neighbor damaged your property
claims.example: Your neighbor's tree fell onto your fence, causing $1,200 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of damage. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Tenant caused damage to rental property
claims.example: A tenant left your rental unit with $3,000 in damages beyond normal wear and tear.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Bring the lease agreement and photos of damages.

claims.name: Damage caused by someone's pet
claims.example: A neighbor's dog dug up your garden, causing $800 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of damage. Provide photos and repair estimates.

claims.name: Damage to property left in someone's care
claims.example: You left your bicycle with a friend, and they returned it with $600 in damages.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from discovery. Document the condition before and after.

claim_categories.title: Landlord-tenant disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Monetary disputes between landlords and tenants up to $5,000 can be addressed in small claims court. Eviction proceedings are excluded.

claims.name: Failure to return security deposit
claims.example: Your landlord withheld your $1,000 security deposit without justification.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Provide the lease agreement and move-out inspection report.

claims.name: Landlord didn't provide itemized deductions
claims.example: Your landlord deducted $500 from your deposit without providing an itemized list of damages.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring correspondence requesting the itemization.

claims.name: Rental unit deemed uninhabitable
claims.example: Your apartment had severe mold issues, making it unlivable, and the landlord refused to address it.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from discovery. Provide photos and health department reports.

claims.name: Landlord kept personal property after move-out
claims.example: After moving out, your landlord refused to return your $2,000 worth of furniture left behind.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Document the items left and any communication.

claims.name: Landlord conducted an illegal eviction
claims.example: Your landlord changed the locks without notice, preventing you from accessing your belongings.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from eviction. Provide evidence of the lockout and any police reports.

claims.name: Landlord didn't return prepaid rent after early termination
claims.example: You paid first and last month's rent but moved out early, and the landlord kept the last month's rent.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from lease termination. Bring the lease agreement and payment records.

claims.name: Tenant failed to pay rent
claims.example: Your tenant owes $4,500 in unpaid rent and has vacated the property.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from breach. Provide the lease agreement and records of missed payments.

claims.name: Tenant repaired and deducted from rent
claims.example: You repaired a broken heater for $600 and deducted it from rent, but the landlord disputes this.
claims.notes: 1-year deadline from repair. Provide repair receipts and communication with the landlord.

claim_categories.title: Employment and wage disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Employees can file claims up to $5,000 for unpaid wages or final paychecks. The statute of limitations is generally 2 years.

claims.name: Employer failed to pay earned wages
claims.example: Your employer owes you $2,000 for hours worked but hasn't paid.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date wages were due. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.

claims.name: Employer didn't provide final paycheck
claims.example: After quitting, your employer withheld your final paycheck of $1,500.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from termination. Document your termination date and owed wages.

claims.name: Unpaid sales commissions
claims.example: Your employer owes you $3,000 in earned commissions but refuses to pay.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date commissions were due. Provide sales records and commission agreements.

claims.name: Employer didn't pay overtime wages
claims.example: You worked 10 hours of overtime but weren't compensated at the overtime rate.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date overtime was worked. Provide timesheets and pay stubs.

claim_categories.title: Personal claims
claim_categories.blurb: Individuals can file claims up to $5,000 for minor injuries or property disputes. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Minor personal injury
claims.example: You slipped on a wet floor in a store, resulting in $2,000 in medical bills.
claims.notes: 2-year deadline from date of injury. Provide medical records and incident reports.

claims.name: Failure to return loaned property
claims.example: You lent a friend your $1,000 camera, and they refuse to return it.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from demand for return. Document the loan agreement and requests for return.

claims.name: Theft of personal property
claims.example: Someone stole your bicycle worth $800, and you know their identity.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of theft. Provide police reports and proof of ownership.

claim_categories.title: Vehicle-specific disputes
claim_categories.blurb: Claims related to vehicles up to $5,000 can be filed in small claims court. The statute of limitations varies by claim type.

claims.name: Dispute over mechanic's charges
claims.example: A mechanic charged you $1,200 for unauthorized repairs.
claims.notes: 5-year deadline from repair date. Provide repair invoices and communication records.

claims.name: Vehicle was wrongfully towed
claims.example: Your car was towed from a legal parking spot, and you incurred $500 in fees.
claims.notes: 3-year deadline from date of tow. Provide towing receipts and photos of the parking area.

exclusions.explanation: Eviction proceedings are not handled in small claims court and must be filed in the district court.
Disputes involving the title to real estate are beyond the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Claims for defamation, including libel and slander, are not permitted in small claims court.
Professional malpractice claims exceed the jurisdictional limits of small claims court.
Issues such as divorce, child custody, and support are not handled in small claims court.
Disputes involving wills and estates are outside the jurisdiction of small claims court.
Small claims court does not have jurisdiction over claims against federal entities.
Class action lawsuits are not permitted in small claims court.
Small claims court cannot issue injunctions or orders for specific performance.
Claims related to workers' compensation are handled by the Industrial Commission.

damages.punitive_explanation: Punitive damages are not permitted in small claims court; only actual damages can be awarded.

statutory_multipliers.explanation: Check amount + service charge up to $40 + civil penalty up to $100 OR 2x check amount, whichever is greater.

damages.attorney_fees_explanation: Attorney fees are not recoverable in small claims court as attorneys are not permitted to represent parties.

where_to_file.court_name: Small Claims Department of the Magistrate's Division

filing_options: Any county where one defendant resides
Where the cause of action arose

where_to_file.consequences_of_wrong_venue: Filing in the wrong venue may result in dismissal or transfer of the case, potentially causing delays and additional costs.
where_to_file.e_filing_notes: E-filing is available through the Idaho Guide & File system for self-represented litigants.

service_fees.notes: Fee for certified mail service in small claims cases.

service_fees.method: Restricted delivery (optional; small claims service only)
service_fees.notes: Optional fee for restricted delivery service in small claims cases.

forms.name: Application for Waiver of Court Fees

eligibility_bullets: Receiving need-based public assistance (SNAP, TANF, SSI)
Household income below 125% federal poverty line
waiver.coverage_notes: The waiver covers filing fees and service fees.
waiver.if_you_win_notes: If you win, the waived fees are added to the judgment against the loser.

fees.fees_recoverable_from_loser_notes: If you win, filing fee and reasonable service costs are added to the judgment.

statute_of_limitations.discovery_rule_notes: The discovery rule applies to fraud claims, starting the clock when the fraud is discovered.
statute_of_limitations.tolling_notes: The statute of limitations may be tolled if the defendant is out of state, the plaintiff is a minor, or in cases of bankruptcy.

pre_filing.demand_letter_notes: While not required, sending a demand letter can often resolve disputes without court intervention.
pre_filing.bad_check_notice_rule: A 30-day written notice is required before pursuing a bad check claim, allowing the issuer to make the check good.
pre_filing.wage_claim_demand_rule: A written demand for unpaid wages is recommended before filing a claim, as it may facilitate resolution.
pre_filing.landlord_tenant_pre_filing_notes: Landlords must provide an itemized list of deductions from the security deposit within 21 days of lease termination.
pre_filing.consumer_protection_notes: Claims under the Idaho Consumer Protection Act require a written demand for relief before filing.

forms.name: Complaint
forms.description: Initiates a small claims case by outlining the plaintiff's claim against the defendant.

forms.name: Summons
forms.description: Notifies the defendant of the lawsuit and the requirement to respond.

forms.name: Answer
forms.description: Allows the defendant to respond to the plaintiff's complaint.

forms.name: Affidavit of Service
forms.description: Proof that the defendant was properly served with the complaint and summons.

forms.name: Request for Default Judgment
forms.description: Filed when the defendant fails to respond, requesting the court to grant judgment in favor of the plaintiff.

forms.name: Satisfaction of Judgment
forms.description: Filed to indicate that the judgment has been paid in full.

forms.name: Notice of Appeal
forms.description: Filed to appeal the small claims court's decision to a higher court.

methods.name: Certified mail by clerk
methods.description: The court clerk sends the summons and complaint to the defendant via certified mail.

methods.name: Sheriff personal service
methods.description: A sheriff or constable personally delivers the summons and complaint to the defendant.

methods.name: Private process server
methods.description: A private individual authorized to serve legal documents delivers the summons and complaint.

service.business_service_rule: Serve the registered agent of the corporation or an officer of the company.
service.out_of_state_defendant_notes: Service must comply with Idaho's long-arm statute and the laws of the state where the defendant resides.
service.cant_find_defendant_notes: If the defendant cannot be located, service by publication may be permitted with court approval.
service.avoiding_service_notes: If the defendant is evading service, document attempts and seek court permission for alternative methods.

response_and_default.response_notes: The defendant must file a written answer within 21 days of service to avoid default judgment.
response_and_default.default_process: If the defendant fails to respond, the plaintiff can request a default judgment by filing the appropriate form.
response_and_default.prove_up_notes: Even if the defendant defaults, the plaintiff must provide evidence to support the claim before judgment is granted.
response_and_default.motion_to_vacate_appeal_notes: A motion to vacate must be filed within 30 days; if denied, an appeal can be filed within 30 days of the denial.

hearing.lawyers_allowed_notes: Attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in small claims court; parties must represent themselves.
hearing.format: Informal bench trial, typically lasting 30-60 minutes.
what_to_bring: Names and contact info for witnesses
hearing.interpreter_request_process: Request an interpreter from the court clerk at least 5 days before the hearing.
hearing.decision_timing: On the spot or mailed within 2 weeks

appeals.type_notes: The appeal is a new trial in the district court, not a review of the small claims court's decision.
appeals.default_judgment_notes: A default judgment cannot be appealed directly; the defendant must first file a motion to vacate the judgment.

collection.post_judgment_interest_notes: Interest accrues on unpaid judgments at a rate of 5% per annum.

methods.name: Wage garnishment
methods.blurb: Have a portion of debtor's wages withheld until the judgment is paid.
methods.description: File a writ of garnishment with the court, serve the employer, and the employer withholds a portion of the debtor's wages to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Most effective when debtor is W-2 employed at a stable job.

methods.name: Bank levy
methods.blurb: Seize funds from debtor's bank account to satisfy the judgment.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, serve the bank, and the bank freezes and transfers funds from the debtor's account.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor has sufficient funds in the account.

methods.name: Property lien
methods.blurb: Place a lien on debtor's real property to secure the judgment.
methods.description: File a certified copy of the judgment with the county recorder to place a lien on the debtor's property.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns real property; lien must be satisfied before property can be sold.

methods.name: Writ of execution
methods.blurb: Authorize the sheriff to seize and sell debtor's non-exempt property.
methods.description: Obtain a writ of execution from the court, and the sheriff seizes and sells the debtor's non-exempt property to satisfy the judgment.
methods.effectiveness_notes: Effective if debtor owns valuable non-exempt property.

collection.judgment_renewal_process: File a motion to renew the judgment before it expires; renewal extends the judgment for another 5 years.

debtor_exam_form.name: Order for Examination of Judgment Debtor

collection.bankruptcy_effect_notes: Filing for bankruptcy imposes an automatic stay on collection efforts; certain judgments may be discharged in bankruptcy.
collection.priority_notes: Priority among multiple judgment creditors is generally determined by the order in which liens are recorded.

businesses.self_representation_notes: Businesses can appear without an attorney in small claims court; an employee may sign pleadings on behalf of the business.
businesses.fictitious_name_notes: Businesses operating under a fictitious name must file a statement with the Secretary of State.
businesses.sole_proprietor_employee_exception: A sole proprietor may authorize an employee to represent the business in small claims court.
businesses.licensing_notes: Unlicensed contractors may be barred from recovering fees for services requiring a license.

pitfalls.title: Wrong defendant naming
pitfalls.what_happens: If you sue 'John's Garage' but the legal entity is 'JG Auto Repair LLC', the judgment may be unenforceable against the LLC's assets.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Search the Secretary of State business records before filing and use the exact registered name; for sole proprietors use the individual's full legal name.

pitfalls.title: Missed statute of limitations
pitfalls.what_happens: Filing one day after the deadline gets the case dismissed with prejudice.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Check the SOL for your specific claim type before filing; for fraud, the discovery rule applies but be prepared to prove when you reasonably could have discovered it.

pitfalls.title: Improper service of process
pitfalls.what_happens: If the defendant isn't properly served, the case may be dismissed or delayed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Follow Idaho's service rules carefully; consider using the sheriff or a professional process server.

pitfalls.title: Failing to appear at hearing
pitfalls.what_happens: If you don't show up, the court may dismiss your case or enter a default judgment against you.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Mark your calendar and set reminders; if you can't attend, notify the court in advance to reschedule.

pitfalls.title: Insufficient evidence
pitfalls.what_happens: Without enough evidence, the court may rule against you even if you're in the right.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: Gather all relevant documents, photos, and witness statements before the hearing.

pitfalls.title: Suing for more than the limit
pitfalls.what_happens: Claims exceeding $5,000 are not allowed in small claims court and will be dismissed.
pitfalls.how_to_avoid: If your claim exceeds the limit, consider waiving the excess or filing in a higher court.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer?
faqs.answer: No, attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in Idaho's small claims court; you must represent yourself.

faqs.question: How long does it take?
faqs.answer: The typical timeline for a small claims case in Idaho is between 30 to 90 days from filing to resolution.

faqs.question: What's the maximum I can sue for?
faqs.answer: You can sue for up to $5,000 in Idaho's small claims court.

faqs.question: What happens if the defendant doesn't show up?
faqs.answer: If the defendant fails to appear or respond, you can request a default judgment; however, you must still provide evidence to support your claim.

faqs.question: Can I appeal?
faqs.answer: Yes, either party can appeal a small claims court decision within 30 days by filing a notice of appeal and paying the required fee.

faqs.question: How long is a judgment good for?
faqs.answer: A judgment in Idaho is valid for 5 years and can be renewed before it expires to extend its enforceability.

state_specific_notes: Idaho's small claims court does not allow counterclaims; defendants must file a separate action if they have a claim against the plaintiff.
Attorneys are not permitted to represent parties in small claims court; self-representation is required.
The maximum amount you can sue for in small claims court is $5,000, including both individuals and businesses.
Filing fees for small claims cases are a flat $69, regardless of the claim amount.
Appeals from small claims court decisions are conducted as a trial de novo in the district court.

interesting_findings: Idaho's small claims court emphasizes informal proceedings to allow individuals to resolve disputes without legal representation.
The prohibition of counterclaims in Idaho's small claims court is unique compared to many other states.
Idaho provides a comprehensive set of forms and instructions to assist self-represented litigants in navigating the small claims process.

sources.label: Idaho Rules of Small Claims Procedure
Service of Process — Idaho Rules of Small Claims Procedure
Idaho Code § 1-2310 (Appeals from Small Claims Department)
Enforcement of Judgment — Idaho Code Title 8, Chapter 3
